title: Pentagon Drops $35B on THAAD, White House Presses Defense CEOs on Stockpile Replenishment
subhead: The U.S. defense industrial base shifted into higher gear on June 24: a $35.3B THAAD contract, an $87.6B supplemental request for the Iran war, and a White House summit with defense CEOs all pointed to a procurement surge. Meanwhile, NATO's Rutte courted Trump ahead of the Ankara summit, India's BrahMos exports expanded, and the MQ-28 Ghost Bat flew its first Pacific exercise.

sections.heading: Lockheed Lands $35.3B THAAD Contract as White House Pushes Munitions Ramp
sections.body:
The Missile Defense Agency awarded Lockheed Martin a $35.3 billion sole-source contract to produce THAAD interceptors through June 2032, with $842.9 million obligated at award. The same Pentagon contract notice included a $398.7 million Raytheon award for AMRAAM missiles, including sales to U.S. allies. The contracts are part of a broader push to quadruple THAAD production and triple Patriot interceptor output.

President Trump met with the CEOs of Boeing, Lockheed Martin, and Honeywell at the White House on June 24, pressing defense firms to accelerate weapons production amid concerns over U.S. missile and munitions stockpiles strained by the Iran campaign and allied transfers. Trump earlier this month invoked the Defense Production Act to speed weapons manufacturing, citing limited production capacity, fragile supply chains, and long lead times. Industry executives have warned that major production increases require sustained congressional funding.

A Senate committee also advanced a bill to codify a Trump executive order requiring Pentagon sign-off before defense contractors can buy back shares or issue dividends — a move defense firms have opposed.
sections.whyItMatters: The scale of the THAAD contract — one of the largest single missile defense awards in decades — signals that the Pentagon is moving from ad hoc replenishment to multi-year procurement commitments. The White House meeting and Defense Production Act invocation put political weight behind that shift, but the funding gap between contract ceiling and obligated amount shows the dependency on congressional appropriations that may not come easy.

sections.heading: White House Requests $87.6B Supplemental, Mostly for Pentagon
sections.body:
The Office of Management and Budget sent Congress an $87.6 billion supplemental spending request on June 24, with $67 billion for the Department of Defense to cover urgent needs related to Operation Epic Fury (the Iran campaign), including military personnel, readiness, and rebuilding weapons stocks. The remaining funds cover agriculture aid, Ebola response, and domestic projects.

The request arrived the same day the Senate rejected a war powers resolution to restrict Trump's authority to wage war against Iran, with the procedural vote failing 50-47. Two Republicans (Collins, Murkowski) voted to advance it; Democrat Fetterman voted no.
sections.whyItMatters: The $67B defense portion is the first full accounting of the Iran war's cost to the Pentagon. The 50-47 war powers vote shows congressional appetite for constraining the president is growing but still short. Together, they frame a debate that will shape defense budgets for years: how much of the supplemental goes to replenishment versus new capability, and whether Congress will attach conditions.

sections.heading: Golden Dome Passes First Live-Intercept Test
sections.body:
Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth announced that the Golden Dome missile defense system successfully completed its first live-intercept milestone test. The classified exercise, conducted at an undisclosed facility, evaluated the system under intense electronic warfare conditions, including the Dynamic Defense Autonomous Defeat system that identified and destroyed simulated drones and cruise missiles without direct human control.

The test also incorporated high-energy lasers, high-power microwave technologies, and prototype space-based sensors to support boost-phase interception. The Golden Dome initiative was launched by executive order in January 2025, modeled conceptually on Reagan's Strategic Defense Initiative but aimed at protecting the entire continental United States.

A Congressional Budget Office estimate projects the complete system could cost up to $1.2 trillion over two decades, far exceeding earlier projections of roughly $175 billion.
sections.whyItMatters: The autonomous intercept capability — machines making kill decisions without human input — is the most significant development here, and it is happening faster than the policy debate around it. The $1.2 trillion CBO estimate will become a reference point for every budget fight over Golden Dome going forward.

sections.heading: NATO's Rutte Courts Trump Before Ankara Summit as Europe's E5 Meets in Berlin
sections.body:
NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte visited the White House on June 24 in what multiple outlets described as a charm offensive ahead of the alliance's July 7-8 summit in Ankara. Rutte brought charts showing European defense spending and investments in the U.S., calling the American defense industrial base 'one of the strongest in the world' and citing over $50 billion in sales to Europe and Canada last year and a roughly $300 billion order book. Trump responded that he was 'disappointed' with most European countries, though he acknowledged European basing was critical for the Iran operation.

Meanwhile, Europe's five largest military powers — France, the UK, Germany, Italy, and Poland — met in Berlin in the E5 format ahead of the summit. Chancellor Merz said all five committed to significantly boost defense spending and coordinate on long-range weapons, air defence, and artificial intelligence. The meeting also addressed the so-called 'coalition of the willing' on Ukraine, with Merz positioning Germany as co-chair alongside France. The UK's role is uncertain after Starmer's resignation as prime minister.
sections.whyItMatters: Rutte's visit was damage control ahead of a summit where Trump may push for European spending targets well above 2%. The E5's agreement on long-range weapons and air defence coordination is a concrete step — if it survives the Ankara summit's political pressures and the UK's leadership transition.

sections.heading: India's BrahMos Export Machine Gains Momentum: Vietnam Deal Confirmed, UAE Talks Advance
sections.body:
A $629 million deal to export BrahMos supersonic cruise missiles to Vietnam has been confirmed, making it India's largest single defense export order. Separately, India and the UAE held strategic defence partnership talks in New Delhi on June 24, with discussions covering the possible sale of both the BrahMos missile and the Akashteer air defence command-and-control network to the Emirates. The UAE is assessing options to strengthen its air and missile defences following attacks on critical installations during the recent West Asia conflict.

India and the UAE signed a strategic defence partnership framework during PM Modi's visit to Abu Dhabi in May 2026. Two Indian Navy warships — including the BrahMos-armed stealth frigate INS Udaygiri — simultaneously concluded a port call in Ho Chi Minh City as part of India's 'Act East' outreach.
sections.whyItMatters: India is moving from being primarily a defense importer to a significant exporter of advanced weapons systems, and the BrahMos is the platform making that happen. The UAE talks matter because they represent India selling to a Gulf state that traditionally buys from the U.S. and Europe — a shift that reflects both the UAE's diversification strategy and India's growing defense industrial credibility.

sections.heading: MQ-28 Ghost Bat Flies in Valiant Shield as CCA Concept Moves from Demo to Exercise
sections.body:
Boeing's MQ-28 Ghost Bat took part in Exercise Valiant Shield 2026 in the Pacific, marking the collaborative combat aircraft's first integration into a large-force multinational exercise. The production-representative test aircraft flew from Rota in the Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands, equipped with an Infra-Red Search and Track (IRST) system. PACAF said the deployment would refine tactics, techniques, and procedures for defensive and offensive counter-air missions, evaluating the MQ-28 as a 'force multiplier' for crewed platforms.

The deployment follows the MQ-28's first U.S. flights in May 2026 and three operational flight tests on the Point Mugu Sea Range. PACAF emphasized that 'a human remains in the loop for all critical decisions,' addressing concerns about autonomous weapons employment.
sections.whyItMatters: The MQ-28 flying in a real Pacific exercise — not a scripted demo — is a significant step for the loyal wingman concept. Every Pacific air force is watching whether CCAs can actually extend manned fighters in contested environments, and this is the first operational data point.

sections.heading: British Army's ASGARD Compresses Corps-Level Planning from 72 Hours to One
sections.body:
General Sir Roly Walker told the RUSI Land Warfare Conference 2026 that the British Army's Project ASGARD has compressed corps-level war planning from 72 hours to one hour, enabling a corps to attack ten times as many targets in a day, limited only by available munitions rather than staff work. Walker described ASGARD as a 'digital juggernaut' evolving every 8-12 weeks.

The UK recently ran ASGARD from a tube station under Trafalgar Square while managing troops in Estonia, processing roughly 10 terabytes of data per day. Walker outlined a future force model of 20% traditional platforms, 40% expendable systems, and 40% reusable AI-driven assets. The British Army is sending thousands of drones to units and fielding 50 operational-level electronic warfare systems drawn from Ukrainian combat experience.

The UK government announced last year it plans to spend £1 billion on AI-enabled military systems. The ASGARD claim comes as Anduril raised $5 billion at a $61 billion valuation and Helsing raised €1.2 billion at an $18 billion valuation, reflecting surging investor appetite for AI-enabled defense platforms.
sections.whyItMatters: A named NATO general putting a hard number on AI-driven planning compression at a major conference changes the procurement conversation across the alliance. If ASGARD's claims hold, every allied army still running corps-level planning on 72-hour cycles has a measurable gap — and vendors like Anduril and Helsing are positioned to fill it.

bottomLine: June 24 marked a decisive shift in U.S. defense procurement posture: a $35.3B THAAD contract, an $87.6B supplemental request, a White House meeting pressuring defense CEOs, and a Defense Production Act invocation all signal that stockpile replenishment after the Iran campaign is moving from discussion to multi-year commitment. The political and financial friction is real — the CBO's $1.2 trillion Golden Dome estimate landed the same day the Senate barely rejected war powers constraints — but the industrial trajectory is clear. Globally, India's BrahMos exports to Vietnam and potential UAE sales, the MQ-28's first Pacific exercise integration, and the British Army's ASGARD claim all point to the same trend: the defense technology market is moving faster than traditional procurement cycles, and governments that cannot adapt their buying processes will fall behind.
sources: CNBC, Reuters, Defense Department contract notices, The Aviationist, RUSI Land Warfare Conference 2026, Startup Fortune, Business Insider, CBS News, Business Standard, The Hindu, USNI News, Stars and Stripes, Daily Times Pakistan, Seeking Alpha, Euronews, Stripes
